name: pg_dump and pg_restore Workflow
description: Create reliable database backups with pg_dump and restore them with pg_restore for disaster recovery and environment cloning.
trendingReason: Backup and restore is the most critical DBA skill and a common interview topic for production readiness
prerequisites: psql and pg_dump available in PATH

steps.title: Create a plain SQL backup
steps.description: Dump the entire database as a SQL script file for portability.
steps.tip: Plain format is human-readable and can be restored with psql. Use -F custom for large databases that benefit from compression.

steps.title: Create a compressed custom-format backup
steps.description: Dump the database in PostgreSQL custom format with built-in compression.
steps.tip: -Z 6 sets gzip compression level (0-9). Custom format also supports parallel restore.

steps.title: Backup a single table
steps.description: Dump only a specific table for targeted backup or migration.

steps.title: Restore from a custom-format backup
steps.description: Restore a database from a custom-format dump file.
steps.warning: The -c flag drops existing objects before recreating them. Omit it if restoring into an empty database.

steps.title: Restore from a plain SQL backup
steps.description: Execute a plain SQL dump file to restore the database.

steps.title: Parallel restore for large databases
steps.description: Speed up restoration by using multiple parallel jobs.
steps.tip: -j 4 uses 4 parallel workers. Match this to your available CPU cores for optimal speed.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# PostgreSQL Backup and Restore
set -euo pipefail

DB_NAME="mydb"
DB_USER="postgres"
BACKUP_DIR="/backups/postgresql"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"

echo "=== Creating custom-format backup ==="
pg_dump -U "$DB_USER" -d "$DB_NAME" -F custom -Z 6 -f "$BACKUP_DIR/${DB_NAME}_${TIMESTAMP}.dump"
echo "Backup: $BACKUP_DIR/${DB_NAME}_${TIMESTAMP}.dump"
ls -lh "$BACKUP_DIR/${DB_NAME}_${TIMESTAMP}.dump"

echo ""
echo "=== Listing backup contents ==="
pg_restore -l "$BACKUP_DIR/${DB_NAME}_${TIMESTAMP}.dump" | head -20

echo ""
echo "=== Backup verification ==="
pg_restore -l "$BACKUP_DIR/${DB_NAME}_${TIMESTAMP}.dump" | wc -l
echo "objects in backup"

echo ""
echo "=== To restore, run: ==="
echo "createdb -U $DB_USER ${DB_NAME}_restored"
echo "pg_restore -U $DB_USER -d ${DB_NAME}_restored -F custom -j 4 $BACKUP_DIR/${DB_NAME}_${TIMESTAMP}.dump"

echo ""
echo "=== Cleanup old backups (30+ days) ==="
find "$BACKUP_DIR" -name "*.dump" -mtime +30 -delete -print

faq.question: Plain vs custom format: which should I use?
faq.answer: Use custom format (-F custom) for production backups. It supports compression, parallel restore, and selective object restoration. Use plain format only when you need a human-readable SQL file or for simple migrations.

faq.question: How do I backup all databases on a server?
faq.answer: Use pg_dumpall to dump all databases, roles, and tablespaces: 'pg_dumpall -U postgres -f /backups/all_databases.sql'. Note that pg_dumpall only outputs plain format.

faq.question: Can I restore specific tables from a custom dump?
faq.answer: Yes. Use 'pg_restore -l dump.file > list.txt' to list contents, edit the list to keep only desired tables, then restore with 'pg_restore -L list.txt -d mydb dump.file'.
